title: Days of the Week & Calendar Tricks

md: ### Days run on mod 7
There are 7 days in a week, so day-of-week arithmetic lives on a 7-clock. If today is Wednesday and you ask 'what day is it 100 days from now?' — that's pure modular arithmetic.
Label the days 0 through 6: Sunday = 0, Monday = 1, ..., Saturday = 6. (Some conventions start at Monday — pick one and stick with it.)
**Then:** if today is day $d$, then $n$ days from now is day $(d + n) \bmod 7$.
### Worked example
Today is Wednesday. What day is it 100 days from now?
$103 = 14 \cdot 7 + 5$, so $103 \equiv 5 \pmod 7$. Day 5 = **Friday**.
Slide the widget above to confirm: the pointer lands on 5.

blocks.title: The bigger reduction trick
blocks.body:
Instead of computing $100 \bmod 7$ all at once, peel off chunks:

$100 = 7 \cdot 14 + 2$

So $100 \bmod 7 = 2$, and $3 + 2 = 5$ → Friday.

In general, **only the remainder mod 7 matters** for day arithmetic. You can throw away whole weeks freely.

md: ### How many days are in a month? (in mod 7)
Modular arithmetic is great for figuring out 'what day of the week is the 1st of next month' given today's date:
**January** has 31 days. $31 \bmod 7 = 3$. So Feb 1 is 3 days later in the week than Jan 1.
**February** (non-leap year) has 28 days. $28 \bmod 7 = 0$. March 1 is the *same day* as Feb 1.
**February (leap year)** has 29 days, so March 1 advances by 1 day.
**April / June / September / November** have 30 days → shift by $30 \bmod 7 = 2$.
**Months with 31 days** → shift by 3.
**Months with 28 days** → shift by 0.
**Months with 29 days** → shift by 1.
### Worked example
If January 1, 2024 was a Monday, what day was March 1, 2024?
**Step 1.** Days from Jan 1 to March 1 = 31 (Jan) + 29 (Feb, leap year) = 60.
**Step 2.** $60 \bmod 7$: $60 = 8 \cdot 7 + 4$, so $60 \equiv 4 \pmod 7$.
**Step 3.** Monday + 4 = Friday.
March 1, 2024 was a Friday. ✓

blocks.title: Year-over-year shifts
blocks.body:
A non-leap year has 365 days = $52 \cdot 7 + 1$. So days of the week shift by **1** every year. Leap years shift by **2**.

If July 4, 2023 (non-leap) was a Tuesday, then July 4, 2024 was a Thursday (shifted by 2 because 2024 is a leap year), and July 4, 2025 will be a Friday (shifted by 1).

This is enough to mentally compute most weekday questions — at least within a decade or two.

md: ### Zeller's congruence (bonus)

There's a closed-form formula that takes any date (day, month, year) and tells you the day of the week, all using $\bmod 7$. It's called **Zeller's congruence**. The formula handles leap years, century rules, etc., and outputs a number 0–6 representing the day. Not memorizable, but exists — and is built entirely on modular arithmetic.
